rulings.Case Title: LOW, BALL & LYNCH, A PROFESSIONAL LAW CORPORATION VS. ANNA GATTI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate And Set Aside Default And Default Judgement And Recall And Quash Writ Of Judgment, If Entered And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT ANNA GATTI's Motion To Vacate And Set Aside Default And Default Judgement And Recall And Quash Writ Of Judgment, If Entered And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons Memorandum Of Points And Authorities. Defendant Gatti's motion to vacate the default entered on April 17, 2018 and default judgment entered on September 27, 2018 is granted. Plaintiff failed to effectuate proper service and the default and default judgment are void pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 473(d). Plaintiff did not properly substitute serve Ms. Gatti at the 690 Market St., #2401 address. Even if she or a related trust owned that property, the undisputed evidence shows that the premises was never her "dwelling house, usual place of abode, usual place of business, or usual mailing address." (Code Civ. Proc. § 415.20(b); Gatti Decl., 7-8, 9, 15.) Plaintiff was on notice that she had moved to Italy in June 2017, and did not request her new mailing address. "[A] default judgment entered against a defendant who was not served with a summons in the manner prescribed by statute is void. Whether the lack of jurisdiction appears on the face of the judgment roll, or is shown by extrinsic evidence for a judgment that appears valid on its face, in either case the judgment is for all purposes a nullity-past, present and future." (OC Interior Services, LLC v. Nationstar Mortgage, LLC (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 1318, 1331 (citations and internal quotations omitted).) Plaintiff properly notes that the service provisions are liberally construed where actual notice has occurred. (See Dill v. Berquist Construction Co. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 1426, 1436.) In contrast, "'[i]n order to obtain in personam jurisdiction through any form of constructive service there must be strict compliance with the requisite statutory procedures.'" (Zirbes v. Stratton (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1407, 1416.) In this case, Ms. Gatti did not receive actual notice of this action in time to defend. (Gatti Decl. 19-22.) The Court therefore grants the motion to vacate and quashes service of the summons and complaint. Order? Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR#8600 email maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JERRY GRIEGO VS. MARK WHITING ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Order Requiring Judicial Arbitration And To Set Case For Trial Setting
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF JERRY GRIEGO's Motion To Vacate Order Requiring Judicial Arbitration And To Set Case For Trial Setting Plaintiff Jerry Griego's motion to vacate order requiring judicial arbitration and to set case for trial setting is denied. Plaintiff has failed to show why he did not comply with the Court's August 8, 2018 order and joint stipulation, which extended the arbitrator's jurisdiction through December 31, 2018. Even assuming the Court has the authority to vacate its prior orders, Plaintiff has not shown good cause to do so. Plaintiff has not presented admissible evidence sufficient to support a conclusion that the amount in controversy will exceed $50,000. Further, Plaintiff did not supplement his discovery responses to refer to his alleged additional injuries and damages. rulings.Case Title: MARK SHENAR VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant City And County Of San Franciscos Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 10, DEFENDANTs CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO and WARREN RUBIT's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication Defendants City and County of San Francisco and Warren Rubit's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendants' motion for summary adjudication as to the first cause of action for negligence is denied. Defendants fail to maintain their initial burden of production. The Court construes the first cause of action as a claim pursuant to Gov't Code § 815.2 ["A public entity is liable for injury proximately caused by an act or omission of an employee of the public entity within the scope of his employment if the act or omission would, apart from this section, have given rise to a cause of action against that employee or his personal representative."]. Defendants fail to show that Mr. Rubit did not breach his duty of care. As a common carrier, defendants owed a heightened duty of care to plaintiff pursuant to Civil Code § 2100. Here, a reasonable trier of fact could conclude that Mr. Rubit negligently pulled plaintiff from the door. The fact that plaintiff thanked Mr. Rubit after he was extricated from the door fails to show that the claim "cannot be established" within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure § 437c(p)(2). Moreover, plaintiff testifies that Mr. Rubit's yanking hurt him and bruised his armpits. (Shenar Depo., 27:9-17.) Plaintiff further stated that the more Mr. Rubit pulled, the more the door squeezed his foot. (Id. at 49:21-25.) Defendants' motion for summary adjudication as to the second cause of action for dangerous condition of public property pursuant to Gov't Code § 835 is granted. To establish this claim, plaintiff must highlight a physical defect with the bus or bus doors. (See Gov't Code § 830; Salas v. Department of Transportation (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 1058, 1070 ["A public entity may be liable for a dangerous condition of public property even where the immediate cause of plaintiff's injury is a third party's negligence if some physical characteristic of the property exposes its users to increased danger from third party negligence . . . . There must be a defect in the physical condition of the property."]; Cerna v. City of Oakland (2008) 161 Cal.App.4th 1340, 1348 ["There must be a defect in the physical condition of the property."].) In this case, plaintiff highlights no such defect and whether Mr. Rubit improperly opened the doors is part of plaintiff's negligence claim. Defendants carried their initial burden of production because plaintiff's special interrogatory responses show that he has no evidence of the existence of a dangerous condition of public property. (Ray Dec., Ex. F, Special Interrogatories 6:5-10:18.) The doctrine of res ipsa loquitur does not apply to the Gov't Code § 835 claim as a matter of law. (Brown v. Poway Unified School Dist. (1993) 4 Cal.4th 820, 836-837.) Seffert v. Los Angeles Transit Lines (1961) 56 Cal.2d 498 is a factually similar negligence case that was not based on Gov't Code § 835. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: NICK VUCUROVICH VS. ROXANNE GOULD ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For A Protective Order For Defendant, Roxanne Gould
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, April 18 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT ROXANNE GOULD's Motion For A Protective Order For Defendant, Roxanne Gould. (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Maxwell Pritt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendant's motion for a protective order and Plaintiffs' request for monetary sanctions are denied. Plaintiff mailed his Form Interrogatories (Set One), Request for Production of Documents (Set One), Request for Admissions (Set One), and Special Interrogatories (Set One) to Victoria Townsend of Hartsukyer, Stratman & Williams-Abrego on November 18, 2019. Pursuant to the Notice of Change of Handling Attorney filed in this case on October 3, 2018, however, Ms. Townsend was no longer Defendant's counsel of record. Accordingly, service of the instant discovery requests was not complete until Defendant's counsel of record accepted service of them on January 23, 2019. Because Defendant's responses were timely, there is no basis for entering a protective order under Cal. Code Civ. Proc. §§ 2030.290 and 2031.300. The Court makes no determination as to the adequacy of Defendant's discovery responses or whether any boilerplate objections warrant sanctions. Pursuant to the Court's inherent power and CCP § 473, the Court sua sponte extends Plaintiff's deadline to move to compel further responses to the instant discovery requests to May 16, 2019. Prior to the initiation of any such motion, the parties must meet and confer, including by telephone, to make "a serious attempt to obtain an informal resolution of each issue." (Townsend v. Super. Ct. (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 1431, 1435 (internal quotation marks omitted); see Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2016.040 ("A meet and confer declaration in support of a motion shall state facts showing a reasonable and good faith attempt at an informal resolution of each issue presented by the motion."); see also Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2023.010(i) ("Misuses of the discovery process include . . . [f]ailing to confer . . . in a reasonable and good faith attempt to resolve informally any dispute concerning discovery . . . .").) (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: BETHANY SHERMAN VS. DOES 1 TO 10
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs, Notice Of Motion, And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Motion
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 11, OTHERs JOHN DOE and JANE DOE's Motion For Attorneys Fees And Costs, Movant John/Jane Doe's motion for attorneys' fees and costs pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.2 is denied. That statute provides in pertinent part, "in making an order pursuant to motion made . . . under section 1987.1, the court may in its discretion award the amount of the reasonable expenses incurred in making or opposing the motion, including reasonable attorney's fees, if the court finds the motion was made or opposed in bad faith or without substantial justification or that one or more of the requirements of the subpoena was oppressive." (Code Civ. Proc. § 1987.2(a).) In contrast to this discretionary provision, section 1987.2(d) makes such an award of attorney's fees mandatory in connection with motions to quash certain subpoenas for personally identifying information for use in an action pending outside California if the underlying action "arises from the moving party's exercise of free speech rights on the Internet and the respondent has failed to make a prima facie showing of a cause of action"; in such cases, "the court shall award the amount of the reasonable expenses incurred in making the motion, including reasonable attorney's fees." (Id. § 1987.2(d) [emphasis added].) When Doe Defendants originally moved to quash the subpoena duces tecum, they also moved for monetary sanctions. As the plain statutory language quoted above makes clear, the trial court had discretion in ruling on that motion to award fees and costs to Doe Defendants. In granting the motion to quash, however, the judge pro tem (to whom both parties stipulated) did not grant Doe Defendants' request for sanctions. Under the circumstances, the judge pro tem must be deemed to have exercised his discretion to deny Doe Defendants' request. Doe Defendants' renewed request to the Court is inconsistent with section 1987.2, which provides that a court may award fees "in making an order" on a motion to quash a subpoena. Further, it does not comply with Code of Civil Procedure section 1008, which provides that a party filing a renewed application for an order a court has previously refused must submit an affidavit showing what "new or different facts, circumstances, or law are claimed" to justify the renewed application, and must show diligence with a satisfactory explanation for not presenting the new or different information earlier. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1008(b), (e); see Evan Zohar Construction & Remodeling, Inc. v. Bellaire Townhouses, LLC (2015) 61 Cal.4th 830, 833.) Finally, this Court lacks the authority to vacate an order made (or, as in this case, refused) by another judicial bench officer in the exercise of his or her discretion. (See People v. Saez (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 1177, 1184-85 ["'For one superior court judge, no matter how well intended, even if correct as a matter of law, to nullify a duly made, erroneous ruling of another superior court judge [improperly] places the second judge in the role of a one-judge appellate court,' and thus 'an order "made in one department during the progress of a cause can neither be ignored nor overlooked in another department . . . ."'"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. TOMAS R MARTINEZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Deem Admissions And Of Nonappearance
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, April 18 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s Motion To Deem Admissions And Of Non appearance. Pro Tem Judge Maxwell Pritt, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's unopposed motion to deem RFAs admitted is granted. Under the Civil Discovery Act, "the court is required to grant a deemed admission motion" if the respondent fails to serve verified responses "before the hearing on that motion." (Tobin v. Oris (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 814, 828, disapproved on other grounds of by Wilcox v. Birtwhistle (1999) 21 Cal.4th 973.) Defendant failed to respond to the RFAs. Any party who contests this tentative ruling must send an email to mpritt@bsfllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm on Wednesday, April 17, 2019 stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative the party contests. The subject line of the email must include the line number, case name, and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative. Plaintiff must notify Defendant by phone of this tentative ruling and of the date, time, and location of the hearing. Plaintiff must also mail or email Plaintiff copies of Code of Civil Procedure sections 2033.210, 2033.220, 2033.230, and 2033.280. Because Plaintiff did not submit a proposed order, Plaintiff must timely comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1312. The Court admonishes Plaintiff to comply with Cal. Rule of Court 3.1112(c) (specifying the information that must be included in the caption of moving papers), and S.F. Super. Ct. Local Rule 2.7 (requiring the moving party to provide courtesy copies to the Court) in connection with any future filings in this case.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ALCANTAR & KAHL, LLP, VS. VIAS ENERGIES, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Vias Energies Inc., A Delaware Corporation'S Petition For Order Compelling Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 12, (part 1 of 2 for tentative ruling) Defendant Vias Energies Inc., A Delaware Corporation'S Petition For Order Compelling Arbitration Defendant Vias Energies Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration is granted and the action is stayed pending arbitration of Plaintiff Alcantar & Kahn, LLP's claims. The parties' retainer agreement contains an arbitration clause which provides that in the event of a dispute as to attorney's fees or costs, Alcantar & Kahn will provide Vias with written notice of its right to arbitrate the dispute under the Mandatory Fee Arbitration Act, Bus. & Prof. § 6200 et seq. Upon receiving such notice, Vias may enter into an agreement "that [such] arbitration will be binding or that the dispute will ultimately be resolved by another form of binding arbitration." In its opposition, Alcantar misleadingly omits the emphasized language. The retainer agreement goes on to provide that "[a]ny other dispute arising under this agreement or in connection with the provision of legal services by us, including, without limitation, any claim for breach of contract, professional negligence, or breach of fiduciary duty, shall be resolved by binding arbitration" under the California Arbitration Act, Code Civ. Proc. § 1280 et seq. Finally, the last paragraph of the arbitration provision removes any ambiguity or uncertainty: "By signing this agreement, you acknowledge that this agreement to arbitrate results in a waiver of your right to a Court or jury trial for any fee dispute or malpractice claim. This also means that you may be giving up your right to discovery and appeal." Here, after Vias declined Alcantar's request that it agree to binding arbitration under the MFAA, Alcantar brought this suit, in contravention of the parties' agreement that any disputes between them would be resolved by binding arbitration under the CAA. Alcantar contends that the MFAA preempts the California Arbitration Act. It is wrong. The California Supreme Court squarely rejected that argument in Schatz v. Allen Matkins Leck Gamble & Mallory LLP (2009) 45 Cal.4th 557. The Court held there, "it is evident the MFAA did not impliedly repeal the CAA. The two statutory schemes do not even govern the same subject. The MFAA concerns nonbinding arbitration that the parties did not agree to in advance, while the CAA concerns binding arbitration agreed to in advance. Further, the two statutes may be rationally harmonized." (Id. at 574.) Under the MFAA, a client may agree in writing to nonbinding arbitration, or after a dispute has arisen may agree to binding arbitration under the same statute. Absent a pre-dispute agreement to nonbinding arbitration, the nonbinding arbitration is optional for the client and mandatory for the attorney if initiated by the client pursuant to Business & Professions Code § 6200(c). However, where an attorney and client have agreed in writing to binding arbitration under the CAA, that agreement is enforceable even if they first engage in arbitration under the MFAA (or, as here, if the parties decline to proceed with arbitration under the MFAA). (Schatz, 45 Cal.4th at 571-72; Rosenson v. Greenberg Glusker Fields Claman & Machtinger LLP (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 688, 693.) (see part 2 of 2 for entire tentative ruling.) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ALCANTAR & KAHL, LLP, VS. VIAS ENERGIES, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Vias Energies Inc., A Delaware Corporation'S Petition For Order Compelling Arbitration (Duplicate Entry For Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 18, 2019, Line 12, (part 2 of 2 for tentative ruling) Defendant Vias Energies Inc., A Delaware Corporation'S Petition For Order Compelling Arbitration (Duplicate Entry For Tentative Ruling) Alcantar has not shown that Vias waived its right to arbitrate under the CAA. Contrary to Alcantar's argument, a client's waiver of MFAA arbitration does not result in a waiver of a CAA arbitration. (Benjamin, Weill & Mazer v. Kors (2011) 195 Cal.App.4th 40, 53 [("[a] contractual right to binding arbitration survives if arbitration under the MFAA is waived or fails to resolve the dispute."].) That Alcantar has retained counsel, filed this lawsuit, and has propounded discovery in this action does not negate Vias's right to compel binding arbitration under the retainer agreement, nor can Alcantar show that it would suffer any cognizable "prejudice" from being required to comply with its own retainer agreement. Vias' request for attorney's fees in connection with the petition to compel arbitration is denied. The Court declines to award fees to the party prevailing on a motion to compel arbitration where there is as yet no prevailing party on the merits of the action as a whole, finding persuasive the reasoning in Frog Creek Partners, LLC v. Vance Brown, Inc. (2012) 206 Cal.App.4th 515, 525-38. (See also DisputeSuite.com, LLC v. Scoreinc.com (2017) 2 Cal.5th 968, 976-977 [citing Frog Creek with approval]; Roberts v. Packard, Packard & Johnson (2013) 217 Cal.App.4th 822, 841-8433 [agreeing with Frog Creek, holding that "the determination of the prevailing parties must await the resolution of the underlying claims by an arbitrator"].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Vias is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
